exercises.name: The 3 Point Rule
exercises.rule: Exactly three supporting points. No more, no less.
exercises.why: Trains you to pick the three things that matter, not list everything.
exercises.objective: Train the discipline of prioritizing exactly three supporting points — stated, supported, and closed one at a time — instead of listing everything.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response does not present exactly three supporting points, structure score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: Pick your three before you open your mouth, then close each one before starting the next. The trap is the sneaky fourth point — the 'oh, and also' that slips in near the end and dilutes the three you chose.
common_failure_modes: A fourth point smuggled in as an 'also' or 'one more thing' near the end
Two points blur into one, so only two distinct arguments actually land
The first point gets most of the airtime, leaving points two and three thin
Points run together with no close, so the listener can't tell where one ends
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether each of the three points is genuinely distinct, gets roughly equal development, and is cleanly closed before the next begins.
exercises.retry_objective: Cut or merge until exactly three points remain, each stated, supported once, and closed before the next begins.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must name a concrete audience and a position with real stakes to defend in a 60-90 second spoken response. Topic and domain vary freely; the exactly-three-points constraint never appears in the prompt text — it is the exercise rule. Prompts should invite more than three possible reasons so the user is forced to prioritize.

prompts.text: Pitch our company's offering to a new prospect.
Convince a skeptical CEO that your team should stay fully remote.
Defend why your favorite book belongs on every reading list.
Convince your team to adopt one shared toolset instead of letting everyone pick their own.
Argue why we should kill the SMB tier and focus on enterprise.
Make the case for hiring a head of design before a head of marketing.
Defend a shift from quarterly to monthly planning cycles.
Argue why our onboarding flow needs a rebuild this quarter.
Justify cutting our paid ads spend in half.
Explain why every PM should learn basic SQL.
Defend why your team should own the data pipeline rather than infra.
Argue why a four-person team will outship a ten-person team this quarter.
Convince a skeptical board to approve a six-month research bet.
Defend a decision to refuse a $2M deal that would distract the roadmap.
Argue why a national hospital system should switch its EHR vendor.

exercises.name: The Story Arc
exercises.rule: Setup, tension, resolution. In that order.
exercises.why: Builds narrative spine — listeners hold story-shaped ideas longer.
exercises.objective: Train building a narrative spine — setup, tension, resolution in order — so any account arrives as a story listeners can hold onto.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response does not move through setup, tension, and resolution in that order, structure score MUST reflect the missing or misordered beat.
exercises.coach_insight: Spend your first sentences making us care how things stood before trouble hit — tension only bites when we know what was at stake. The trap is opening mid-crisis, or ending on what happened instead of what changed.
common_failure_modes: Opens in the middle of the crisis with no setup, so the stakes never register
Tension mentioned in one flat sentence rather than built and felt
Resolution collapses to 'and it worked out' with no consequence or lesson
A chronological list of events with no audible beat changes between them
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the tension genuinely escalates out of the setup and whether the resolution pays off that specific tension rather than tacking on a generic ending.
exercises.retry_objective: Rebuild the response around the weakest beat so setup, tension, and resolution each land, in that order.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a first-person account with a clear listener and a reason they are hearing it, suited to a 60-120 second spoken response. Topic varies across work and life scenarios, but every prompt must contain latent tension so a setup-tension-resolution arc is available. The prompt never dictates the arc itself — finding it is the exercise.

prompts.text: Tell me about a project that almost failed but didn't.
Walk me through the most stressful week of your career.
Tell the story of a time you had to sell something — an idea, a product, yourself — and almost lost the sale.
Recount the moment you realized a big part of your life needed to change.
Walk me through a time technology failed you at the worst moment and what it took to fix it.
Tell the story of a deal or plan that fell apart at the eleventh hour.
Describe a time something you made or organized flopped, and what you did the next morning.
Walk me through a time you picked the wrong person for a job or task, start to finish.
Tell the story of a customer, client, or supporter who showed up exactly when you needed them most.
Walk me through a time a routine change you made caused far more chaos than you expected.
Recount a time you turned down an offer or deal, then found yourself reconsidering.
Describe a time you struggled to teach someone something until it finally clicked.
Imagine you're a physician: tell a colleague how one clinical trial upended the way you treat patients.
Imagine you're a CEO briefing your board: walk through the quarter revenue collapsed, then recovered.
Imagine you're an executive: recount the board meeting where you had to defend your most unpopular call.

exercises.name: Bottom Line First
exercises.rule: Conclusion in sentence one. No warmup.
exercises.why: Earns the listener's attention before you explain how you got there.
exercises.objective: Train leading with the full conclusion in sentence one so everything after it supports a point the listener already has.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the conclusion is not in sentence 1, structure score MUST reflect the lack of bottom-line-first framing.
exercises.coach_insight: Make your literal first words the decision itself — 'Promote internally' — then spend the rest earning it. The trap is the disguised warmup: 'Great question, there are a few factors here' is still burying the answer.
common_failure_modes: Opens with context, background, or 'it depends' before any answer appears
First sentence names the topic or the tradeoff, not an actual conclusion
Hedges the opening ('I can see both sides') and only commits near the end
Leads with one answer but drifts to a different conclusion by the close
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the first sentence heavily: is it the full, committed conclusion, and does every sentence after it work in service of that stated answer.
exercises.retry_objective: Move the conclusion into sentence one and re-deliver with zero warmup, context-setting, or buildup before the point.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must pose a genuine either/or or yes/no decision from a specific stakeholder with real stakes, answerable in 60-90 seconds. The decision topic varies; every prompt must have a defensible answer that can be stated in sentence one so warmup is never necessary. The prompt asks the question — it never tells the user to answer first.

prompts.text: Should we hire externally or promote internally for the VP role?
Is now the right time to ask for a raise?
Should we ship the feature with known bugs or delay a week?
Buy or build the analytics layer?
Should we accept the acquisition offer or stay independent?
Do we fire the underperforming director or coach them another quarter?
Should we open the European office this year or wait?
Migrate to the new cloud provider or stay with the incumbent?
Should we kill the mobile app and double down on web?
Cut the bottom 10% of accounts to free up CSM bandwidth?
Your doctor offers two paths: surgery now or watchful waiting. Which do you choose?
Should the school adopt a four-day week next year?
Raise a Series B now or wait twelve months for better metrics?
Should we settle the lawsuit or take it to trial?
Pull the drug from the market or issue a label change?

exercises.name: The Problem Solution Frame
exercises.rule: Name the problem. Then name the fix.
exercises.why: Forces you to ground every proposal in the pain it solves.
exercises.objective: Train grounding every proposal in the pain it solves by naming the problem plainly before offering the fix.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response does not name the problem before presenting the fix, structure score MUST reflect the missing problem framing.
exercises.coach_insight: Give the problem its own plain sentence — with the number and who it hurts — before you touch the fix. The trap is racing to your solution so fast the pain never gets named, leaving the fix floating with nothing to justify it.
common_failure_modes: Jumps straight into the solution without ever stating the problem
Parrots the prompt's symptom without framing why it hurts or who feels it
Proposes a fix that answers a different problem than the one named
Problem and fix interleaved throughout, so neither is stated cleanly once
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the fit between problem and fix: a crisply named pain up front and a solution that maps directly onto that pain, not onto a nearby one.
exercises.retry_objective: Open by naming the problem in one plain sentence, then deliver a fix that maps directly onto that pain.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must state a concrete, ideally quantified pain and ask the speaker to address it in front of a stakeholder who feels it, in 60-90 seconds. The pain and domain vary; the prompt always supplies the symptom but never the fix — framing the problem and proposing the solution is the user's job.

prompts.text: Our onboarding has a 40% drop-off in week one. Talk about it.
Standups are running 45 minutes. Address it.
Engineering and design keep shipping conflicting designs. Solve it.
Your inbox has 4,000 unread messages. Walk me through your plan.
Customer support tickets jumped 60% last month. Fix it.
Sales reps aren't following the playbook. Address it.
Our cloud bill doubled this quarter with no traffic growth. Talk about it.
Senior engineers won't mentor juniors. Address it.
Three of your top five customers asked the same missing-feature question. Respond.
Patient wait times in the ED have crept past four hours. Fix it.
Test scores dropped a full grade level across the district. Address it.
Your team's PR review turnaround is now six days. Solve it.
Net retention slipped below 100% for the first time. Talk about it.
A whistleblower memo just leaked to the press. Address it.
Two co-founders disagree on whether to pivot. Resolve it.

exercises.name: Monroe's Motivated Sequence
exercises.rule: Attention, need, solution, visualization, action — every time.
exercises.why: A persuasion scaffold built for moving an audience to act.
exercises.objective: Train moving a listener from awareness to action through all five persuasion steps — attention, need, solution, visualization, action — in order.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if any of the five steps (attention, need, solution, visualization, action) is missing or out of order, structure score MUST reflect the incomplete sequence.
exercises.coach_insight: Give all five steps their own audible beat — especially painting what life looks like after they say yes. The trap is a vivid problem and fix that fizzles into 'so, something to think about' instead of one concrete ask.
common_failure_modes: Skips the visualization beat, jumping from solution straight to the ask
Opens with the request itself instead of something that earns attention first
Closes on a vague 'let's discuss' rather than one specific, doable action
Frames the need as the speaker's problem rather than the audience's
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the back half of the sequence — the painted after-picture and the concrete closing ask — where speakers most often let the persuasion collapse.
exercises.retry_objective: Re-deliver hitting all five steps in order, giving the skipped or thinnest step its own explicit beat.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask the speaker to move a named audience to one specific action, with stakes high enough that persuasion is genuinely required, in a 60-120 second spoken response. Audience and cause vary; the call to action must always be concrete enough to close on. The five-step sequence stays out of the prompt text — it is the constraint.

prompts.text: Convince the team to adopt a four-day work week.
Persuade your family to take a week-long camping trip.
Rally a book club into committing to a six-month reading challenge.
Convince a junior engineer to apply for a stretch role.
Persuade the exec team to invest in a customer advisory board.
Rally an engineering team to adopt shared coding standards across every project.
Convince sales to drop a deeply discounted legacy SKU.
Persuade finance to fund a six-month platform rewrite.
Rally the office to take mental-health days seriously.
Convince a hospital board to invest in a telehealth program.
Persuade a school district to pilot AI tutoring this fall.
Rally citizens to support a new local transit referendum.
Convince the board to approve a hostile counter-offer this week.
Persuade a regulator to accept a novel drug delivery pathway.
Rally a workforce of 2,000 to accept a return-to-office mandate.

exercises.name: Signpost First
exercises.rule: Announce your structure before delivering it.
exercises.why: Gives listeners a mental table of contents so they don't get lost.
exercises.objective: Train announcing the structure up front so listeners get a mental table of contents before the content arrives.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response does not announce its structure before delivering it, structure score MUST reflect missing signposting.
exercises.coach_insight: Hand over the map before you drive — 'Three steps: assess, fix, verify' — then say each name again as you arrive at it. The trap is announcing one roadmap and delivering another; that's worse than no roadmap at all.
common_failure_modes: No upfront roadmap — the structure only emerges as the answer unfolds
Announces parts but delivers a different count or order than promised
Gives the roadmap once, then never echoes it, so listeners lose their place
Announces five-plus parts, more than any listener can hold in mind
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight fidelity between announcement and delivery: matching count and order, and transitions that echo the labels promised up front.
exercises.retry_objective: Open by announcing the parts you will cover, then deliver them in exactly the announced order.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask for a plan, process, or approach delivered to a specific listener in 60-90 seconds — material with natural parts to announce. Topic varies from technical to personal; every prompt must decompose into two to four announceable steps. The prompt never asks for the announcement itself — supplying the roadmap is the exercise.

prompts.text: Walk me through how you'd plan a product launch.
Explain how you'd run your first one-on-one with a new direct report.
Describe your approach to learning a new programming language in a month.
Walk through how you'd plan a weekend that recharges you.
Explain how you'd debug a slow production database.
Walk me through the steps to take a feature from idea to GA.
Describe how you'd structure a year-one finance org from scratch.
Explain your process for resolving a conflict between two senior leaders.
Walk through how you'd run an incident postmortem.
Describe your approach to evaluating a new market entry.
Walk me through how you'd treat a new patient with vague chest pain.
Explain how you'd redesign a chaotic curriculum.
Walk through how you'd integrate two engineering orgs post-acquisition.
Describe your approach to taking a private company public.
Walk through how you'd respond to a zero-day affecting all customers.

exercises.name: Two Then One
exercises.rule: Two supporting beats, then one payoff.
exercises.why: Builds anticipation: the listener feels the third hit land harder.
exercises.objective: Train sequencing two supporting beats before one payoff so the final point lands with maximum weight.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response does not deliver two supporting beats followed by one closing payoff, structure score MUST reflect the broken two-then-one shape.
exercises.coach_insight: Decide before you speak which argument is your closer, and protect it — the first two beats just set the table. The trap is spending your best material first, so the ending trails off exactly where it should hit hardest.
common_failure_modes: Strongest argument spent first, leaving a weak point to close on
Three or four equal-weight points with no distinguishable payoff
The payoff lands but is diluted by trailing afterthoughts and caveats
Supporting beats that never build toward or connect to the final point
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the final third: whether the last point lands as the clear peak of the argument and the response stops there instead of trailing past it.
exercises.retry_objective: Hold the strongest point for last — deliver two supporting beats, then land the single payoff and stop.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must ask the speaker to make a case to a named decision-maker with real budget, roadmap, or people stakes in 60-90 seconds. Topic varies; every prompt must support at least three viable arguments so the user chooses which two build and which one lands the payoff. The two-then-one shape never appears in the prompt text.

prompts.text: Make the case for raising our pricing 20%.
Argue why your team deserves a bigger headcount budget.
Convince a friend to take up running.
Sell me on attending the offsite next month.
Argue why we should sunset our oldest product line.
Make the case for moving QA earlier in the lifecycle.
Argue why customer success should report into product, not sales.
Convince the CFO to approve a brand refresh.
Make the case for hiring a chief of staff this year.
Argue for moving from open offices to focused rooms.
Convince the medical board to fund a new fellowship track.
Make the case for adopting standardized rubrics across grade levels.
Argue why we should bet the company on a single AI platform.
Convince a foundation to fund a ten-year research program.
Make the case for taking on debt to acquire a competitor.

exercises.name: Compare and Contrast
exercises.rule: Force an A vs B spine through the whole answer.
exercises.why: Sharpens thinking — defining by contrast is more vivid than by description.
exercises.objective: Train holding an A-versus-B spine through the entire answer so the position is defined by contrast rather than description.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response drops the A vs B comparison spine at any point, structure score MUST reflect the abandoned contrast frame.
exercises.coach_insight: Pick two or three criteria and run both options through each one — 'On cost, A does this; B does that.' The trap is describing A in full, then B in full, and never actually setting them side by side on the same point.
common_failure_modes: Describes each option in its own block with no point-by-point contrast
Drops one option midway and spends the rest advocating for the favorite
Criteria shift between options, so points about A and B never pair up
Ends without resolving the contrast into a position or recommendation
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether each major point pits A against B on the same criterion, and whether the contrast survives to the end instead of collapsing into one-sided advocacy.
exercises.retry_objective: Re-deliver keeping both options alive in every beat — each point about A answered by the parallel point about B.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must name two concrete options (A vs B) inside an implied decision context with stakes, answerable in 60-90 seconds. The option pair varies across domains; both sides must be genuinely defensible so contrast, not a foregone winner, carries the answer.

prompts.text: The tool your team knows inside out vs the newer one everyone's excited about.
Working from home vs working from the office.
Reading fiction vs reading nonfiction for personal growth.
Bootstrapping vs raising venture capital for an early-stage startup.
Inside sales vs field sales for a mid-market product.
Microservices vs a well-structured monolith at our stage.
Promote from within vs hire a seasoned outside leader.
Quarterly OKRs vs continuous goal-setting.
In-house design team vs an external agency.
Coaching vs replacing an underperforming executive.
Telehealth vs in-person care for chronic disease management.
Project-based learning vs direct instruction in K-12.
Going horizontal vs staying vertical with our platform.
A defensive merger vs an aggressive solo IPO path.
Open-sourcing the core vs guarding it as a moat.

exercises.name: Question Then Answer
exercises.rule: Open with the listener's question. Then resolve it.
exercises.why: Hooks attention by speaking the question they were already asking.
exercises.objective: Train hooking attention by opening with the question the listener is already asking, then structuring the rest as its resolution.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response does not open by voicing the listener's question before resolving it, structure score MUST reflect the missing question-first hook.
exercises.coach_insight: Voice the exact question your listener is silently asking — worded the way they would say it — then make every sentence pay it down. The trap is opening with the question and then answering a different, easier one.
common_failure_modes: Opens with background or a flat statement instead of voicing the question
Restates the question, then drifts into an adjacent question by mid-answer
Offers loosely related observations that never resolve the opening question
Voices the question in stiff, essay-like phrasing no real listener would use
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the bond between opening and body: a naturally voiced question up front, and everything after it working to resolve exactly that question.
exercises.retry_objective: Open by speaking the question the listener is already asking, then spend the rest of the rep resolving exactly that question.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must be phrased as the exact 'why' question a curious listener would ask, with an implied audience who cares about the answer, in 60-90 seconds. Topic varies; every prompt must be a question worth restating aloud as the opening hook, and the resolution must stay on that one question.

prompts.text: Why do most strategy decks fail?
Why is it so hard to stick with a new habit?
Why do good engineers quit good companies?
Why do most New Year's resolutions die by February?
Why do free trials so rarely turn into paying customers?
Why do reorgs almost always disappoint?
Why do AI features ship demos but fail in production?
Why do strong teams still miss quarter after quarter?
Why is customer churn the metric founders ignore the longest?
Why do most M&A deals destroy value?
Why do clinical guidelines lag the latest evidence by years?
Why do well-funded edtech tools rarely move student outcomes?
Why do founders cling to dying business models?
Why is institutional trust collapsing in so many countries at once?
Why does scientific fraud still slip through peer review?
